blurb: A tough, silvery-leaved garden hybrid with flat-topped canary-gold flower heads that bloom relentlessly from early summer into fall — xeric, pollinator-friendly, deer-resistant, and at home in Front Range sun and alkaline soil.
spread: Slowly spreading clump; divide every 2–3 years to keep vigor
seasons: Silver-grey ferny foliage from spring through fall; flat golden-yellow corymbs Jun–Sep; foliage semi-evergreen in mild Front Range winters; seedheads add minor winter texture
wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — attracts native bees, beneficial wasps, hoverflies, and butterflies; larval host for some moth species
deer: Deer-resistant; strong aromatic foliage deters browsing; also rabbit-resistant
toxic: Mild — aromatic foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals (wear gloves); toxic to cats, dogs, and horses if ingested in quantity

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8; extremely cold-hardy and reliable on the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). The silvery foliage often remains semi-evergreen through mild Boulder winters, greening up reliably in early spring.
care.planting: Plant in-ground in spring (April–May) or early fall (September). Well-drained soil is essential — yarrow planted in wet, compacted clay will rot over winter. Amend heavy clay with coarse grit or pea gravel, or site on a raised bed. In containers, use a well-drained mix; containers dry out quickly in Front Range heat and wind, so check moisture every few days in summer. Overwinter containers in a sheltered spot or sink the pot into the ground with a light mulch over the rim — roots in pots are less cold-hardy than in-ground crowns.
care.sun: Full sun is required for compact growth and maximum bloom — 6+ hours of direct sun daily. Partial shade causes stretchy, floppy stems that require staking, and reduces flower production.
care.soil: Average to lean, well-drained soil; tolerates clay if drainage is adequate, sandy and gravelly substrates, and the alkaline pH common to Front Range soils (6.0–8.0). Avoid rich, constantly moist, heavily amended soils — they produce lush, floppy, short-lived plants prone to crown rot.
care.water: Low — drought-tolerant once established, typically getting by on 12–15 in of annual moisture. Water moderately during the establishment season (first year); in subsequent years, deep watering every 2–3 weeks during summer heat is usually sufficient. Overwatering is the primary cause of failure.
care.spacing: 18–24 in apart; plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form patches 18–24 in wide. Divide every 2–3 years to prevent the center from dying out and to maintain vigorous flowering.
care.propagation: Easiest by division in early spring (April–May) or early fall (September): lift the clump, discard the woody center, and replant vigorous outer sections with roots attached — a reliable and fast method. Also propagates from softwood basal cuttings in early summer. Seeds are viable but 'Moonshine' is a hybrid cultivar, so seedlings may not match the parent (off-type flower color or foliage silvering); division is the preferred method to maintain cultivar characteristics.
care.bloom: June through September on the Front Range — a long, canary-gold display of flat-topped corymbs (4–5 in across) held above the silvery foliage. Deadheading spent heads promptly encourages rebloom and extends the season; stop deadheading in late August to allow some seedhead development for minor winter interest and bird value.
care.feeding: Minimal — one light application of a balanced, slow-release fertilizer in spring is optional if soil is very poor. On typical Front Range soils, no feeding is needed; excess nitrogen produces floppy stems and reduces flowering.
care.maintenance: Deadhead regularly throughout summer for continuous bloom. Cut the whole plant back by half to two-thirds after the first main flush (late June–July) to promote a second wave of flowering. In fall after frost, cut stems back to a few inches; the silver basal rosette often persists through winter. Divide every 2–3 years in spring when the center begins to thin or die out.
care.troubles: Powdery mildew in humid summers — ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering. Stem rot in wet or poorly drained soil. Aggressive spreaders in rich, moist soil; lean conditions keep spread modest. Aphids occasionally on new growth. Contact dermatitis possible — wear gloves when handling.
care.companions: Russian sage, catmint, salvia, coneflower, ornamental grasses (little bluestem, blue grama), ornamental alliums, penstemon, and any other full-sun xeric or drought-tolerant perennials; the silver foliage is a superb foil for blue and purple flowers.
